---
title: ""
author: "Giorgio Arcara"
date: ""
#format: revealjs
format:
  beamer:
    theme: metropolis
    keep-tex: false
incremental: false
header-includes:
  \setbeamerfont{title}{size=\large}
  \usepackage{hyperref}
  \setbeamertemplate{footline}[frame number]
  \usepackage{tikz}
  \usetikzlibrary{arrows.meta, positioning, shapes.geometric}
  \usetikzlibrary{overlay-beamer-styles}
---

<!-- the section below is shown only if rendering is beamer from latex. (and format:revealjs is commmented) -->

::: {.content-visible when-format="beamer"}
```{=latex}
% TITLE SLIDES
\title{{\fontsize{15pt}{16pt}\selectfont Applied Psychometrics}:\\ studying subclinical psychopathologies\\ \vspace{1.5em} \raggedright 
\emph{6.2 - Test-Retest Reliability}}
\author{Giorgio Arcara,\\ University of Padua \\ IRCCS San Camillo, Venice}

\titlegraphic{

\vspace*{7cm}
\includegraphics[scale=0.15]{Figures/LogoSanCamilloIRCCS_Unipd_alpha.png}
\begin{center}
\vspace{-2.2em}
\includegraphics[scale=0.15]{Figures/CC_license_3_0.png}
\end{center}
}
\date{2026}
\maketitle
```

## A very old scale

![](Figures/old_scale.png){fig-align="center" width="50%"}

## Reliability and Measurement Error

$X = T + E$
\vfill

$X$ = Observed Score

$T$ = True Score

$E$ = Measurement Error

\vfill
\small
Assumption of Classical Test Theory: If we measure repeatedly _the same thing_, and then we average, the Measurement Error, assumed to be random, will cancel out.

## Test-Retest reliability
```{r}
#| echo: FALSE
#| output: TRUE
#| fig.dpi: 100
#| fig.width: 7
#| fig.height: 5

library(ONDAutils)

set.seed(10)
X = simulate_obs(Ts = 10, n = 30, rel=0.8, mu_true = 8, sd_true = 2, label = "Observed and True Score")
abline(h=10, col="blue", lwd=3)
legend("topright", legend=c("observed score", "true score"), pch=c(1, NA), lty=c(NA, 1), lwd=c(NA, 3), col=c("black", "blue"))

```

## High reliability


```{r} 
#| echo: FALSE
#| output: TRUE
#| fig.dpi: 100
#| fig.width: 7
#| fig.height: 5

set.seed(10)
X = simulate_obs(Ts = 10, n = 30, rel=0.9, mu_true = 8, sd_true = 2, label = "High Reliability")
abline(h=10, col="blue", lwd=3)
legend("topright", legend=c("observed score", "true score"), pch=c(1, NA), lty=c(NA, 1), lwd=c(NA, 3), col=c("black", "blue"))

```

## Low reliability


```{r} 
#| echo: FALSE
#| output: TRUE
#| fig.dpi: 100
#| fig.width: 7
#| fig.height: 5

set.seed(10)
X = simulate_obs(Ts = 10, n = 30, rel=0.4, mu_true = 8, sd_true = 2, label = "Low Reliability")
abline(h=10, col="blue", lwd=3)
legend("topright", legend=c("observed score", "true score"), pch=c(1, NA), lty=c(NA, 1), lwd=c(NA, 3), col=c("black", "blue"))

```


## How is it estimated?

- Participants with _no expected change_ are tested twice at a given interval.

- This interval may depend on the clinical context and should be justified (both conceptually and practically).


## Problems of Test-Retest Reliability

- There are infinite test-retest reliability values \pause(depending on the interval)

- for performance tests, **practice effects** can have an important influence!

\pause

## How is test-retest reliability estimated?

Test-retest reliability is assessed through two main analyses:

- Correlation Coefficient (between pairwise measurements)

- Intraclass Correlation Coefficient
\vfill


**Recommended value**: $\geq 0.70$ or $\geq 0.80$ 

!!! But see also next group slides _6.3 Assessing Reliability_ !!!


## Practice effect

```{r}
#| echo: FALSE
#| output: TRUE
#| fig.dpi: 100
#| fig.width: 7
#| fig.height: 5


set.seed(100)
n = 30
Ts = 10
mu_true = 15
sd_true = 1
rel = 0.8
Xs = simulate_obs(Ts = Ts, n = n, rel=rel, mu_true = mu_true, sd_true = sd_true, plot_res=FALSE) # no practice effect

pe = 0.1
Xs_pe = Xs + pe*(1:length(Xs))

df = data.frame(N = 1:n, Xs=Xs, Xs_pe = Xs_pe) # create version with practice effect (simple and linear)



par(mfrow=c(1,2), mar=c(2,2,5,2))

plot(df$N, df$Xs, ylim=c(0, 20), xlab="Observation", ylab="Score", main = "No Practice Effect")
lines(df$N, df$Xs)
abline(h=Ts, col="blue", lwd=3)
legend("topright", legend=c("observed score", "true score"), pch=c(1, NA), lty=c(NA, 1), lwd=c(NA, 3), col=c("black", "blue"))


plot(df$N, df$Xs_pe, ylim=c(0, 20), xlab="Observation", ylab="Score", main = "(Linear) Practice Effect")
lines(df$N, df$Xs_pe)
abline(h=Ts, col="blue", lwd=3)
legend("topright", legend=c("observed score", "true score"), pch=c(1, NA), lty=c(NA, 1), lwd=c(NA, 3), col=c("black", "blue"))



```
## A common mistake
![](Figures/triangle.png){fig-align="center" width="20%"}
Test-retest reliability is **always** important for a test, non only in the case of repeated measurement!

\pause
Remember the example with the scale at the beginning of these slides.

## Before moving on
\centering
Can practice effect influence are estimate of Test-Retest reliability?

A small exercise
